mnemonics: A sparse matrix is like a STAR: Sparse Tables Are Rare. Only non-zero entries are worth storing, just like only stars are visible in a vast, empty night sky.
Use the CRS method: Remember the order as Count, then Row, then Store. First count the non-zero elements per row for the row pointer, then store the column index and value.
keyPoints: A sparse matrix is one where the majority of elements are zero, making standard 2D array storage inefficient.
Common storage formats include Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) and Compressed Sparse Column (CSC), which only store non-zero values and their locations.
The primary advantage of using a sparse matrix representation is a massive reduction in memory usage and faster computation for operations like matrix-vector multiplication.
commonMistakes: Using a dense matrix representation for a sparse matrix, wasting immense memory and processing power on zero values.
Incorrectly updating the row pointer array in the CSR format when modifying the matrix, which corrupts the entire data structure.
Assuming all matrix operations (like accessing an element by index) will have the same time complexity as a dense matrix; in formats like CSR, element access can be O(n) instead of O(1).
